setting.note: Janie Crawford's search for love and a self of her own carries her across Florida through three marriages: from her grandmother's cabin and a loveless farm in the west, to the all-Black town of Eatonville where she is the mayor's silenced wife, to the Everglades 'muck' by Lake Okeechobee where she finds joy with Tea Cake, until the great 1928 hurricane. Hurston roots her landmark of Black American literature in real places she knew, above all the self-governing town of Eatonville and the migrant bean fields of the Glades.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Zora Neale Hurston, first published 1937. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Their Eyes Were Watching God, Zora Neale Hurston (1937). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The green of the blossoming pear tree under which Janie first dreams of love, the image of desire and selfhood the whole book reaches toward. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
